content.method: Value-added modeling using longitudinal student-level competency assessment data with propensity-score or regression-discontinuity designs around admission cutoffs, disaggregated by region, urbanization, and prior socioeconomic status; replicate Gurzó & Horn (2015) with updated cohorts and labor-market outcome data.
content.question: Does the ministerial claim that 6/8-year gimnázium students show no value-added learning advantage over peers hold up once measurement limitations and student-composition confounding are controlled for, and how large is any genuine heterogeneity in track effects across regions and prior-achievement groups?
content.why_it_matters: This shapes the case for T1 (admissions redesign, which presumes tracks have pedagogical value worth sorting fairly for) versus T3/T4 (which presume tracks are largely credentialing/sorting devices); if effects are null on average but heterogeneous, targeted rather than blanket reform may be warranted.
